annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

tools.description: Close a GitHub issue with a closing comment. Use this when work is complete, the issue is no longer relevant, or it's a duplicate. The closing comment should explain the resolution or reason for closing. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be closed. Target: *.

body.description: Closing comment explaining why the issue is being closed and summarizing any resolution, workaround, or conclusion.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to close. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 901 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/901). If omitted, closes the issue that triggered this workflow (requires an issue event trigger).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

state_reason.description: The reason for closing the issue. Use 'COMPLETED' for resolved issues, 'NOT_PLANNED' for issues that won't be addressed, or 'DUPLICATE' for duplicate issues. Defaults to 'COMPLETED'.

tools.name: close_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Target: *.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be added. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository. This field is required — omitting it will cause a validation error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be removed. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to remove labels from. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: remove_labels

tools.description: Assign one or more GitHub users to an issue. Use this to delegate work to specific team members. Users must have access to the repository.

assignee.description: Single GitHub username to assign. Use 'assignees' array for multiple users.

assignees.description: GitHub usernames to assign to the issue (e.g., ['octocat', 'mona']). Users must have access to the repository.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to assign users to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 543 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/543). If omitted, assigns to the issue that triggered this workflow.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: assign_to_user

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Post a routing comment @mentioning team owners on the triggering issue; bypasses safe-outputs mention neutralization

message.description: The comment body text without any @mentions or @ symbols

owners.description: Comma-separated GitHub usernames to notify, without the @ prefix (e.g. 'user1, user2, Azure/team-name')

tools.name: mention_owners

content.text: {"number":57937,"title":"[ComputeSchedule] Docs: Add code snippets for VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex and wire README examples","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule` **Service directory:** `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/` **Triggered by:** commit [REDACTED] (PR #57188) by `@azure-sdk` (co-authored by `@Asifmammadov89`) ## What Changed PR #57188 added support for the `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` API operation (introduced in API version `2026-03-01-preview`). This operation enables on-demand flexible VM provisioning with Spot or regular priority, configurable VM size profiles, OS type, and allocation strategy. The PR added scenario tests for this operation but no README documentation or test-backed code snippets. ## Gaps Found - The README **Examples** section only links to a generic management samples page — it has no package-specific code snippets for any of the ComputeSchedule operations - The new `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` operation has no sample code or documentation showing how to use it (required parameters, priority type enum, VM size profiles, allocation strategy, inline network config) - None of the existing sample files in `tests/Samples/` use `#region Snippet:` / `#endregion` markers, so **no samples are wired up** to appear in the README via the snippet injection system - The README already mentions `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` in the intro but does not demonstrate usage with a code example 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files **File:** `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/README.md` — **Edit** Replace the current minimal Examples section: ```markdown ## Examples Code samples for using the management library for .NET can be found in the following locations - [.NET Management Library Code Samples]((aka.ms/redacted) ``` With an expanded section that includes placeholder blocks for the snippet tool to inject: ```markdown ## Examples Code samples for using the management library for .NET can be found in the following locations - [.NET Management Library Code Samples]((aka.ms/redacted) ### Execute an immediate VM start ``` ``` ### Execute flexible VM creation (VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex) ``` ``` ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets #### Snippet 1 — Execute start **Test file:** `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/tests/Samples/Sample_SubscriptionResourceExtensions.cs` Wrap an existing `VirtualMachinesExecuteStart` test method body with a named region (or add a new dedicated sample method): ```csharp [Test] [Ignore("Only validating compilation of examples")] public async Task ExecuteStart_Sample() { #region Snippet:ComputeSchedule_ExecuteStart ArmClient client = new ArmClient(new DefaultAzureCredential()); SubscriptionResource subscription = await client.GetDefaultSubscriptionAsync(); var resources = new ExecuteResourceOperationContent( new ResourceOperationDetails( new UserRequestRetryPolicy(RetryCount.One, RetryWindowInMinutes.Thirty), new List { new ResourceId("/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/{vmName}") }), new CorrelationId(Guid.NewGuid().ToString())); Response response = await subscription.VirtualMachinesExecuteStartAsync( AzureLocation.EastUS, resources); Console.WriteLine($"Operation result: {response.Value.Type}"); #endregion } ``` **README location:** Under the `### Execute an immediate VM start` heading added in Step 1. **Snippet name:** `ComputeSchedule_ExecuteStart` --- #### Snippet 2 — ExecuteCreateFlex **Test file:** `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/tests/Samples/Sample_SubscriptionResourceExtensions.cs` Add a new sample method: ```csharp [Test] [Ignore("Only validating compilation of examples")] public async Task ExecuteCreateFlex_Sample() { #region Snippet:ComputeSchedule_ExecuteCreateFlex ArmClient client = new ArmClient(new DefaultAzureCredential()); SubscriptionResource subscription = await client.GetDefaultSubscriptionAsync(); var flexRequest = new ExecuteCreateFlexContent( new CreateFlexDetails( new List { new VmSizeProfile("Standard_D2s_v3") { Rank = 1 }, new VmSizeProfile("Standard_D4s_v3") { Rank = 2 } }, OSType.Windows, AllocationStrategy.Prioritized, new InlineNetworkInterfaceConfiguration( new SubnetConfiguration("/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/{vnet}/subnets/{subnet}"))), new CorrelationId(Guid.NewGuid().ToString())) { PriorityType = PriorityType.Spot }; Response response = await subscription.VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlexAsync( AzureLocation.EastUS, flexRequest); Console.WriteLine($"Created VMs: {response.Value.Results.Count}"); #endregion } ``` **README location:** Under the `### Execute flexible VM creation (VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex)` heading added in Step 1. **Snippet name:** `ComputeSchedule_ExecuteCreateFlex` ### Step 3: Verify README structure The README at `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/README.md` currently contains all required top-level sections. The **Examples** section (gap #1 above) needs to be fleshed out with actual snippet placeholders as described above. All other sections are present but reference to `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` should remain in the intro. Use `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md` as the canonical reference for section formatting and tone. ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order. Each must succeed before proceeding to the next: 1. `dotnet build sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 computeschedule` — check the git diff afterward to confirm README snippet blocks were updated with the sample code 4. `eng/scripts/Export-API.ps1 computeschedule` — only required if public API surface changed; in this case the API was already added, so verify no new API listing changes are needed 5. `dotnet format sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24144050834/agentic_workflow) · ● 484.7K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57937","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-08T15:43:09Z","updated_at":"2026-04-08T15:43:09Z"}

label 'Compute Schedule' not found in Azure/azure-sdk-for-net

arguments.body:
## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Summary:** This bot-filed issue identifies a documentation gap in the `Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule` package — specifically, missing code snippets for the `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` operation and lack of wired-up README examples via the snippet injection system.

<details>
<summary>📋 Issue Details</summary>

**Package:** `Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule` (SDK directory: `sdk/computeschedule/`)
**Affected API:** `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` (API version `2026-03-01-preview`), `VirtualMachinesExecuteStart`
**Scenarios:**
- README Examples section only links to a generic management samples page — no package-specific code snippets for any ComputeSchedule operations
- `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` has no sample code demonstrating required parameters, priority type, VM size profiles, allocation strategy, or inline network config
- Existing sample files in `tests/Samples/` lack `#region Snippet:` / `#endregion` markers, so no samples are wired up to appear in README via the snippet injection system

**Root ask:** Add `#region Snippet:` wrappers to sample methods and expand the README Examples section with concrete code snippets for `VirtualMachinesExecuteStart` and `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex`.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes</summary>

The issue was generated automatically following PR #57188 (commit `[REDACTED]`), which added `VirtualMachinesExecuteCreateFlex` and scenario tests, but no README documentation or snippet-tagged samples.

**Suggested investigation steps:**
1. Review `sdk/computeschedule/Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule/README.md` to confirm the Examples section only contains a generic link.
2. Check `tests/Samples/Sample_SubscriptionResourceExtensions.cs` for the absence of `#region Snippet:` / `#endregion` markers.
3. Add snippet markers to existing test methods and new `ExecuteCreateFlex_Sample` method as described in the Implementation Guide.
4. Run `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 computeschedule` and verify the README is updated with injected code blocks.
5. Run `dotnet build` and `dotnet test --filter TestCategory!=Live` to confirm compilation and tests pass.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🏷️ Label Confidence</summary>

- **Category:** `Mgmt` — The package is `Azure.ResourceManager.ComputeSchedule`, which clearly falls under the management library category.
- **Service:** Unknown — Neither `Compute Schedule` nor `ComputeSchedule` exists as a label in this repository. No CODEOWNERS entry was found for this service, and no quality reference issues (with both `customer-reported` and `issue-addressed` labels) were found for this package.
- **Confidence:** Low — Category is clear (`Mgmt`), but no matching service label exists in the repo, preventing a confident paired label prediction. Applied `needs-triage` for manual review.
</details>
